did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with MobileNet architecture and a CNN-SVM hybrid model to classify seven types of human skin diseases using the HAM10000 dataset. The study explicitly employs deep learning for feature extraction and machine learning (SVM) for classification to obtain diagnostic results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work comparing the performance of CNN and CNN-SVM models for skin disease classification. It details specific methods (MobileNet architecture, SVM kernels), a dataset (HAM10000 with 21,000 images), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy of 93.47% for CNN). Although it includes a literature review section, the primary contribution is the empirical validation and comparison of proposed classification systems, not a summary of prior literature.

title: Comparative Analysis of CNN and CNN-SVM Methods For Classification Types of Human Skin Disease
journal: Sinkron : Jurnal dan Penelitian Teknik Informatika
brief_summary: This study compares the performance of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with MobileNet architecture and a CNN-Support Vector Machine (SVM) hybrid model for classifying seven types of human skin diseases. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, augmenting the original 10,015 images to a total of 21,000 images across seven classes. The CNN model achieved a testing accuracy of 93.47%, with precision, recall, and F1-scores of 93.55%, 93.74%, and 93.62%, respectively. In contrast, the CNN-SVM model, tested with poly, rbf, linear, and sigmoid kernels, demonstrated lower performance, with the best testing accuracy reaching only 81% using the poly kernel. The findings indicate that the standalone CNN model outperforms the CNN-SVM hybrid approach for this specific multi-class skin disease classification task.

all_task_types.target: Seven types of human skin diseases (akiec, bbc, bkl, df, mel, vasc, nv)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin diseases using CNN with MobileNet architecture and CNN-SVM hybrid models on the HAM10000 dataset. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories for 7 classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, downloaded from the Harvard Dataverse website. The original dataset contained 10,015 images, which were augmented to 21,000 images for this study.

use.training: 80% of the augmented dataset (16,800 images) was used to train the CNN and CNN-SVM models.
use.validation: 10% of the augmented dataset (2,100 images) was used to evaluate model performance during training.
use.testing: 10% of the augmented dataset (2,100 images) was used to assess the final performance of the models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the CNN and CNN-SVM models were based solely on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

CNN_Model_MobileNet.notes: Model trained with 80% data, validated with 10%, tested with 10%. Ran for 50 epochs, batch size 64, learning rate 0.001, SGD optimizer. Lowest training loss 0.005, lowest validation loss 0.26.

CNN_SVM_Model_RBF_Kernel.notes: Other metrics (precision, recall, F1) not explicitly reported for RBF kernel in the provided text, only accuracy values.

conclusions: The standalone CNN model with MobileNet architecture outperformed the CNN-SVM hybrid model in classifying seven types of human skin diseases from the HAM10000 dataset.
The CNN model achieved a testing accuracy of 93.47%, with precision, recall, and F1-scores of 93.55%, 93.74%, and 93.62%, respectively.
The CNN-SVM model demonstrated lower performance than the standalone CNN, with its best testing accuracy reaching only 81% when using the poly kernel.

Among the CNN-SVM configurations tested, the poly kernel yielded the highest performance (81% accuracy), while the rbf kernel achieved 79% accuracy; linear and sigmoid kernels were also tested but specific metrics were not detailed in the results summary.
The study acknowledges that CNN models have limitations, including the need for large and representative datasets and long training times.
The authors suggest that researchers can enhance CNN-SVM model performance by utilizing varied kernel types and techniques for complex feature representations.
Data augmentation was critical in balancing the dataset, increasing the total image count from 10,015 to 21,000 across seven classes to address class imbalance issues.
important_remarks: The CNN-SVM model used a different data split (90% training, 10% testing) compared to the standalone CNN model (80% training, 10% validation, 10% testing), making direct performance comparison potentially biased due to differing training data volumes.

The study addresses class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by artificially augmenting all seven classes to exactly 3,000 images each (total 21,000), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence distributions.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance difference between the CNN and CNN-SVM models.
Specific quantitative metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for the CNN-SVM models using linear and sigmoid kernels were not reported in the results.

The CNN model achieved 100% accuracy on the 'df' (Dermatofibroma) and 'nv' (Melanocytic Nevi) classes, which may indicate potential data leakage or overfitting given the perfect scores on these specific classes.
The research relies solely on the HAM10000 dataset without external validation on independent datasets, limiting the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper acknowledges that CNN models require large datasets and have long training times, but does not report computational cost metrics (e.g., inference time, FLOPs) for deployment feasibility.
